---
title: "Subgradients: Calculus for Functions with Kinks"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Optimization"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article,
# matched to the rest of the series.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

FUNC   = "#2b3a67"    # the function itself
SUPP   = "#c1121f"    # a supporting line / subgradient
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"    # highlights, optima, the subdifferential interval
GREY   = "#6c757d"
FILL   = "#9db4d4"    # shaded regions
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the third and final article of the
> **convexity spine** of the Optimization track. It assumes
> [OPTM-04 *Convex Functions*] — in particular the **first-order / tangent-below
> characterization** of a *differentiable* convex function,
> $f(y)\ge f(x)+\nabla f(x)^\top(y-x)$, the single inequality this whole article
> generalizes. From [OPTM-02 *Minimizing in Many Variables*] it borrows the **gradient**
> $\nabla f$ and the stationarity condition $\nabla f(x)=\mathbf 0$, whose non-smooth
> replacement we are about to build; no new cross-track machinery beyond that gradient is
> needed. Its single job is to extend "set the derivative to zero" to convex functions
> that *have no derivative* at certain points — functions with **kinks**, like $|x|$ at
> the origin and the lasso penalty $\|\boldsymbol\beta\|_1$. It **supplies** the
> *subgradients* prerequisite requested by the Regression track (R15, the lasso), and it
> sets up [OPTM-10 *Coordinate Descent*] (whose per-coordinate lasso update is the
> **soft-thresholding** rule derived here) and [OPTM-16 *Proximal and Projected Methods*]
> (whose $\ell_1$ proximal operator *is* that same soft-threshold).

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

For a smooth convex function, "you are at the minimum" is captured by one equation,
$\nabla f(x)=\mathbf 0$: the tangent is flat. But the functions optimization cares about
most are often *not* smooth — the absolute value $|x|$, the hinge loss
$\max(0,1-t)$, the lasso penalty $\|\boldsymbol\beta\|_1$ all have **kinks**, points
where no single tangent exists. This article repairs the gap. We notice that the
defining inequality of a convex function — *the graph lies above every tangent line* —
never actually needed the tangent to be unique. At a kink there is not one supporting
line but a whole **fan** of them, and each one's slope is a legitimate stand-in for the
derivative: a **subgradient**. The set of all of them is the **subdifferential**
$\partial f(x)$. We define it precisely, compute it for the cases that matter — $|x|$
(the interval $[-1,1]$ at $0$), the general piecewise-linear convex function, and the
$\ell_1$ norm — and prove the calculus rules (scaling, sums, the chain rule for affine
maps) that let us assemble subdifferentials of complicated objectives from simple parts.
The payoff is the **subgradient optimality condition** $\mathbf 0\in\partial f(x^\star)$,
the exact non-smooth analogue of $\nabla f=\mathbf 0$, which we prove is *necessary and
sufficient* for a global minimum of a convex function — no second-order test required.
We cash it in by solving the scalar lasso problem
$\min_\beta \tfrac12(\beta-\rho)^2+\lambda|\beta|$ in closed form, deriving
**soft-thresholding**, the per-coordinate update behind every lasso solver. We close on
the honest boundary: a subgradient is *not* a descent direction, so naive subgradient
descent crawls and chatters — which is precisely why the field reaches for proximal
methods instead. The single claim this article defends is that **the kink is not a defect
to be smoothed away but a first-class object with its own clean calculus**, and that
calculus is what makes sparse modeling computable.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
A **subgradient** of a convex function $f$ at a point $x$ is any vector $g$ that makes
the tangent-below inequality hold:
$$
f(y)\;\ge\; f(x) + g^\top(y-x)\qquad\text{for all }y.
$$
It is a slope whose line passes through $(x,f(x))$ and stays *under* the whole graph. Where
$f$ is differentiable there is exactly one such slope, $g=\nabla f(x)$. **At a kink there
are many**, and the set of all of them is the **subdifferential** $\partial f(x)$ — a
closed convex set, not a single vector. The minimum is no longer "the slope is zero" but
"**zero is one of the allowed slopes**":
$$
x^\star \text{ minimizes } f \iff \mathbf 0 \in \partial f(x^\star).
$$
For convex $f$ this is *necessary and sufficient* and global — the entire reason the
condition is worth having. Applied to the lasso it produces **soft-thresholding**, the
update that makes coefficients exactly zero.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The first inequality looks like a mild relaxation of a
familiar fact; by the end you should see that dropping the word *"the"* (the tangent) in
favor of *"a"* (a supporting line) is exactly the move that lets calculus survive contact
with the absolute value — and that the sparsity of the lasso is a direct consequence of
the subdifferential of $|x|$ being a fat interval $[-1,1]$ rather than a single number.

# Why we are here: calculus stops at the corner {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **how do we say "you cannot do better"
at a point where the function has no derivative?** Every definition and rule below is an
answer to a piece of that question. If a step feels arbitrary, come back here.

## The problem the smooth theory cannot touch

The Optimization track's spine so far has run on derivatives. [OPTM-01] located a 1-D
minimum by $f'(x)=0$; [OPTM-02] generalized it to $\nabla f(x)=\mathbf 0$; [OPTM-04]
showed that for a *convex* function that stationary point is automatically the *global*
minimum — the headline guarantee the rest of the series leans on. Every one of those
statements assumes the function is differentiable.

But look at what we actually minimize in practice. The single most important
regularizer in modern statistics is the **lasso penalty**, $\lambda\|\boldsymbol\beta\|_1
= \lambda\sum_j|\beta_j|$, added to a regression loss to force coefficients to zero and
produce a sparse, interpretable model (Regression R15). The **hinge loss**
$\max(0,1-y\,t)$ that defines the support vector machine has a corner at $t=1/y$. The
$\ell_1$ and $\ell_\infty$ distances, the $\max$ of several linear functions that appears
in robust and minimax formulations, the total-variation penalty in image denoising — all
**convex**, all **non-differentiable** at exactly the points where the action happens.

The absolute value is the cleanest specimen. @fig-kink draws $f(x)=|x|$: a perfect convex
V with a sharp corner at the origin. To its left the slope is $-1$; to its right it is
$+1$; *at* the origin there is no slope at all, because the left and right derivatives
disagree. So the question "where is $f'(x)=0$?" has no answer — and yet the picture makes
the minimum obvious. The bottom of the V is at $x=0$. The smooth theory cannot *say* that.
That gap is the entire reason this article exists.

```{python}
#| label: fig-kink
#| fig-cap: "The canonical kink. $f(x)=|x|$ is convex — a straight segment between any two points on the graph stays on or above it — but it is not differentiable at $x=0$, where the left slope ($-1$) and the right slope ($+1$) disagree. The smooth condition $f'(x)=0$ has no solution, yet the minimizer is plainly $x=0$. We need a notion of 'derivative' that survives the corner and still pinpoints that bottom."
x = np.linspace(-2.2, 2.2, 400)
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(x, np.abs(x), color=FUNC, lw=2.4, label=r"$f(x)=|x|$")
# left and right tangents meeting at the kink
ax.plot([-2.2, 0], [2.2, 0], color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.3)
ax.plot([0, 2.2], [0, 2.2], color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.3)
ax.annotate("slope $-1$", xy=(-1.3, 1.3), xytext=(-2.0, 1.9), fontsize=10, color=GREY)
ax.annotate("slope $+1$", xy=(1.3, 1.3), xytext=(0.7, 1.9), fontsize=10, color=GREY)
ax.scatter([0], [0], color=SUPP, s=70, zorder=5)
ax.annotate("kink: no\nunique slope", xy=(0, 0), xytext=(0.25, 0.75),
            fontsize=10, color=SUPP)
ax.set_xlabel("$x$"); ax.set_ylabel("$f(x)$")
ax.set_title("A convex function with no derivative at its minimum")
ax.legend(loc="upper center", framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why not just smooth the kink — replace $|x|$ with $\sqrt{x^2+\epsilon}$ — and use ordinary
calculus?"* You can, and people do; it is called **smoothing** or the **Huber
approximation**, and it is a legitimate tactic. But it has two real costs. First, the
sparsity is *gone*: the whole point of the lasso is that it sets coefficients to *exactly*
zero, and a smoothed penalty has a unique nonzero minimizer for every input, so it only
shrinks, never zeroes (we prove this contrast in @sec-softthresh). The exact zeros come
*from* the kink — smoothing it away destroys the feature you wanted. Second, you have
traded an exactly-solvable problem for an approximately-solvable one and introduced a
nuisance parameter $\epsilon$ that trades accuracy against conditioning. Subgradient
calculus lets you keep the kink, keep the sparsity, and *still* write down a clean
optimality condition. That is the better deal, and it is what this article delivers.
:::

## The historian's note

The idea is due to the **convex-analysis** school of the 1960s — **Werner Fenchel**,
**Jean-Jacques Moreau**, and above all **R. Tyrrell Rockafellar**, whose 1970 *Convex
Analysis* is still the reference. They were not chasing the lasso (which arrived in 1996);
they were building a complete calculus for convex functions that did not flinch at
non-differentiability, because non-smooth convex functions arise unavoidably the moment
you take a $\max$, an $\ell_1$ norm, or the support function of a set. The motivating *why*
is therefore older and deeper than any single application: **convex functions are closed
under operations that create kinks** — pointwise maximum, sums of norms, indicator
functions of constraint sets — so a calculus that only worked for smooth convex functions
would be a calculus that broke on its own most natural constructions. Subgradients are what
make convex analysis closed under its own operations. Decades later, the lasso, the SVM,
total-variation denoising, and compressed sensing turned this "pure" machinery into the
daily workhorse of statistical learning.

# Intuition before symbols: lines that stay underneath {#sec-intuition}

Before any formula, hold one picture. [OPTM-04] taught the **tangent-below** property of a
differentiable convex function: at any point, the tangent line lies *on or below* the
entire graph. The graph curves away upward from its own tangents; that is what convexity
*is*, read first-order.

Now stare at that picture and ask: *what was special about the tangent?* It was the unique
line through $(x,f(x))$ that touches without crossing. But at a smooth point that line is
unique only because the graph has a single well-defined slope there. **At a kink the graph
has no single slope**, and now there is not one supporting line but a whole *fan* of them,
all pivoting through the corner, all staying under the graph. @fig-fan shows the fan at the
bottom of the V: every line through the origin with slope between $-1$ and $+1$ sits under
$|x|$. Each such slope is just as good a "first-order model" of the function at that point
as the tangent was at a smooth point. There is no reason to prefer one over another, and no
reason to throw them away.

```{python}
#| label: fig-fan
#| fig-cap: 'The fan of supporting lines at a kink. At $x=0$, every line $y=gx$ with slope $g\\in[-1,1]$ passes through the corner $(0,0)$ and lies entirely on or below $f(x)=|x|$ (shaded). Each such slope $g$ is a **subgradient**: a legitimate stand-in for the missing derivative. The whole interval of admissible slopes, $[-1,1]$, is the **subdifferential** $\\partial f(0)$. At a smooth point the fan collapses to a single line — the ordinary tangent.'
x = np.linspace(-2.2, 2.2, 400)
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.fill_between(x, np.abs(x), 2.3, color=FILL, alpha=0.25)
ax.plot(x, np.abs(x), color=FUNC, lw=2.6, label=r"$f(x)=|x|$", zorder=4)
for g in np.linspace(-1, 1, 9):
    ax.plot(x, g * x, color=SUPP, lw=1.0, alpha=0.7)
ax.scatter([0], [0], color="k", s=55, zorder=6)
ax.annotate("fan of supporting\nlines, slopes in $[-1,1]$",
            xy=(0.9, 0.0), xytext=(0.55, 1.5), fontsize=10, color=SUPP,
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color=SUPP, lw=1.2))
ax.set_ylim(-0.5, 2.3)
ax.set_xlabel("$x$"); ax.set_ylabel("$y$")
ax.set_title("Every slope in $[-1,1]$ supports the V from below")
ax.legend(loc="upper center", framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

That is the entire idea, in one sentence: **a subgradient is the slope of any line that
touches the graph at $x$ and stays underneath it everywhere.** Where the function is
smooth there is exactly one — the gradient. Where it kinks there are many, and we keep all
of them. The minimum condition then becomes intuitive in advance of any proof: a point is
the bottom of a convex function exactly when a *flat* line (slope $0$) is among the lines
that support the graph there — because a flat supporting line says "everything else is at
least as high." For $|x|$ at the origin, $0$ is squarely inside the fan $[-1,1]$, so the
origin is the minimum. The smooth theory could not say that; the fan can.

# Formal development {#sec-formal}

## The subgradient and the subdifferential {#sec-def}

We now make the picture precise. We work with a convex function
$f:\mathbb R^n\to\mathbb R$. (Everything specializes to $n=1$, the picture above, by
reading vectors as scalars and $g^\top(y-x)$ as $g\,(y-x)$.)

::: {#def-subgradient}
**Definition (subgradient, subdifferential).** A vector $g\in\mathbb R^n$ is a
**subgradient** of $f$ at $x$ if
$$
f(y)\;\ge\; f(x) + g^\top(y-x)\qquad\text{for all }y\in\mathbb R^n.
$$ {#eq-subgrad}
The **subdifferential** of $f$ at $x$, written $\partial f(x)$, is the set of *all*
subgradients of $f$ at $x$:
$$
\partial f(x) \;=\;\bigl\{\,g\in\mathbb R^n : f(y)\ge f(x)+g^\top(y-x)\ \text{for all }y\,\bigr\}.
$$ {#eq-subdiff}
:::

Read @eq-subgrad against the tangent-below inequality of [OPTM-04],
$f(y)\ge f(x)+\nabla f(x)^\top(y-x)$. They are *the same inequality*, with the single
forced choice $g=\nabla f(x)$ relaxed to *any $g$ that works*. The right-hand side
$f(x)+g^\top(y-x)$ is the **affine function** (a flat plane in $n$ dimensions, a line in
1-D) that equals $f$ at $x$ and has slope $g$; the inequality demands that this plane lie
on or below the graph of $f$ everywhere. We call such a plane a **supporting hyperplane**
of the graph at $x$, or a **global affine minorant** that is *tight* at $x$ (it touches
the graph there). A subgradient is exactly the slope of such a plane.

Three facts about $\partial f(x)$ follow immediately from the definition, and we prove
each because they are used constantly.

::: {#lem-subdiff-closed-convex}
**Lemma (the subdifferential is closed and convex).** For any $f$ and any $x$, the set
$\partial f(x)$ is a closed, convex subset of $\mathbb R^n$ (possibly empty).
:::

::: {.proof}
Fix $x$. For each fixed $y$, the condition "$f(y)\ge f(x)+g^\top(y-x)$" is a single
inequality that is **linear in $g$**: writing $a_y = y-x$ and $b_y = f(y)-f(x)$, it reads
$a_y^\top g \le b_y$. The set of $g$ satisfying one such inequality is a closed half-space
(or all of $\mathbb R^n$ if $a_y=0$). The subdifferential @eq-subdiff is the set of $g$
that satisfy this for *every* $y$ simultaneously — that is, the **intersection** over all
$y$ of these half-spaces:
$$
\partial f(x) = \bigcap_{y\in\mathbb R^n}\bigl\{\,g : a_y^\top g \le b_y\,\bigr\}.
$$
An intersection of closed half-spaces is closed (each is closed; an arbitrary intersection
of closed sets is closed) and convex (each half-space is convex; an arbitrary intersection
of convex sets is convex — proved as the intersection-preservation property in
[OPTM-03]). Hence $\partial f(x)$ is closed and convex. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This is why the subdifferential of $|x|$ at $0$ came out as an *interval* $[-1,1]$ and not
some scattered set of slopes: closed convex subsets of the line $\mathbb R$ are exactly
closed intervals (possibly infinite, possibly a single point). The fan of @fig-fan had to
be an interval, and now we know why.

::: {#lem-subdiff-gradient}
**Lemma (smooth points: the subdifferential is the gradient).** If $f$ is convex and
differentiable at $x$, then $\partial f(x) = \{\nabla f(x)\}$ — a single point.
:::

::: {.proof}
Two directions. **($\nabla f(x)\in\partial f(x)$.)** Convexity plus differentiability gives
the tangent-below inequality of [OPTM-04], which is precisely @eq-subgrad with
$g=\nabla f(x)$. So the gradient is a subgradient.

**(Nothing else is.)** Suppose $g\in\partial f(x)$, so $f(y)\ge f(x)+g^\top(y-x)$ for all
$y$. Take $y = x + t d$ for a fixed direction $d$ and a scalar $t>0$. The inequality gives
$f(x+td) - f(x) \ge t\,g^\top d$, so dividing by $t>0$,
$$
\frac{f(x+td)-f(x)}{t} \;\ge\; g^\top d.
$$
Let $t\downarrow 0$. The left side tends to the directional derivative, which for a
differentiable $f$ equals $\nabla f(x)^\top d$ (the definition of differentiability — see
[OPTM-02]). So $\nabla f(x)^\top d \ge g^\top d$, i.e. $(\nabla f(x)-g)^\top d\ge 0$. This
holds for *every* direction $d$. Now also use $d$ replaced by $-d$: it gives
$(\nabla f(x)-g)^\top(-d)\ge 0$, i.e. $(\nabla f(x)-g)^\top d\le 0$. The two together force
$(\nabla f(x)-g)^\top d = 0$ for all $d$, and a vector orthogonal to *every* direction is
the zero vector. Hence $g=\nabla f(x)$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

So subgradients genuinely *extend* the gradient: they agree with it wherever the gradient
exists, and only add new content at the kinks. Nothing is lost. The last basic fact we need
is that for a convex function the subdifferential is never empty in the interior of the
domain — there is always *at least one* supporting line.

::: {#lem-nonempty}
**Lemma (existence of a subgradient).** If $f:\mathbb R^n\to\mathbb R$ is convex (and
finite everywhere, as all our examples are), then $\partial f(x)\neq\varnothing$ for every
$x$.
:::

::: {.proof}
We give the one-dimensional argument in full and flag the $n$-dimensional step as imported.
**In 1-D**, everything rests on one fact about convex functions — that **chord slopes
increase as you slide to the right** — so we prove that first, from the definition of
convexity itself, and then build the right and left derivatives on top of it.

*The three-point (increasing-slope) lemma.* Let $f:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R$ be convex and
take any three points $a<b<c$. We claim the slope of the left chord is at most the slope of
the right chord:
$$
\frac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}\;\le\;\frac{f(c)-f(b)}{c-b}.
$$ {#eq-three-chord}
To see it, write the middle point $b$ as a weighted average of the outer two. With
$\theta = \frac{c-b}{c-a}\in(0,1)$ we have $\theta a + (1-\theta)c = b$ (check:
$\theta a+(1-\theta)c = a + (1-\theta)(c-a) = a + \frac{b-a}{c-a}(c-a) = b$). The defining
**chord-above-graph** inequality of a convex function from [OPTM-04],
$f(\theta a+(1-\theta)c)\le \theta f(a)+(1-\theta)f(c)$, then reads
$$
f(b)\;\le\;\frac{c-b}{c-a}\,f(a)+\frac{b-a}{c-a}\,f(c).
$$
Multiply through by $(c-a)>0$ and group: $(c-a)f(b)\le (c-b)f(a)+(b-a)f(c)$. Now subtract
$(c-a)f(b)$-worth of terms to isolate each chord. Write $c-a=(c-b)+(b-a)$ on the left, so
the inequality becomes $(c-b)f(b)+(b-a)f(b)\le (c-b)f(a)+(b-a)f(c)$, i.e.
$(c-b)\bigl(f(b)-f(a)\bigr)\le (b-a)\bigl(f(c)-f(b)\bigr)$. Dividing by the positive number
$(b-a)(c-b)$ gives exactly @eq-three-chord. The same chain, kept as a three-way grouping,
also yields the companion inequalities
$\frac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}\le \frac{f(c)-f(a)}{c-a}\le \frac{f(c)-f(b)}{c-b}$ (the outer chord
slope sits between the two inner ones); we use the two-sided form below.

*The right derivative exists.* Fix $x$ and look at the difference quotient
$q(t)=\frac{f(x+t)-f(x)}{t}$ for $t>0$. For $0<t_1<t_2$ the three points
$x<x+t_1<x+t_2$ are increasing, and the outer-chord inequality
$\frac{f(b)-f(a)}{b-a}\le\frac{f(c)-f(a)}{c-a}$ (with $a=x$, $b=x+t_1$, $c=x+t_2$) reads
$q(t_1)\le q(t_2)$ directly. So $q$ is **nondecreasing in $t>0$** — larger steps really do
see steeper average slopes, and now we have shown it rather than asserted it. It is also
bounded below: applying @eq-three-chord with a point $a'<x$ fixed, $b=x$, $c=x+t$ shows
every $q(t)$ is at least the backward chord slope $\frac{f(x)-f(a')}{x-a'}$, a finite
constant. A function of $t>0$ that is nondecreasing in $t$ and bounded below has a finite
limit as $t\downarrow 0$ — namely its infimum $\inf_{t>0}q(t)$ — so the **right derivative**
$$
f'_+(x) = \lim_{t\downarrow 0}\frac{f(x+t)-f(x)}{t}
$$
exists and equals $\inf_{t>0}q(t)$. The mirror argument on $t<0$ (where the quotient is
nondecreasing as $t\uparrow 0$ and bounded above) gives the **left derivative** $f'_-(x)$.
Finally, applying @eq-three-chord with $a=x-s$, $b=x$, $c=x+t$ ($s,t>0$) gives
$\frac{f(x)-f(x-s)}{s}\le\frac{f(x+t)-f(x)}{t}$; letting $s\downarrow 0$ and $t\downarrow 0$
yields $f'_-(x)\le f'_+(x)$ — proved, not assumed.

*Every $g\in[f'_-(x),f'_+(x)]$ is a subgradient.* Take $y>x$ and set $t=y-x>0$. Because
$q(t)\ge\inf_{s>0}q(s)=f'_+(x)$ (the right derivative is the infimum), the chord slope
satisfies $\frac{f(y)-f(x)}{y-x}\ge f'_+(x)\ge g$, so $f(y)-f(x)\ge g(y-x)$. Take $y<x$;
the analogous fact $\frac{f(y)-f(x)}{y-x}\le f'_-(x)\le g$ holds (the left difference
quotient is bounded above by its limit $f'_-(x)$), and since $y-x<0$, multiplying by it
flips the inequality to $f(y)-f(x)\ge g(y-x)$ again. Either way @eq-subgrad holds, so
$g\in\partial f(x)$ and the interval $[f'_-(x),f'_+(x)]$ is nonempty.
**In $n$ dimensions** the existence of a supporting hyperplane to the convex
**epigraph** of $f$ (the region on and above the graph, a convex set by [OPTM-04]) at the
boundary point $(x,f(x))$ is exactly the **supporting-hyperplane theorem**, the
$n$-dimensional sibling of the separating-hyperplane fact established in [OPTM-03]; its
normal vector, normalized, yields a subgradient. We import that one geometric fact rather
than rebuild it. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The 1-D proof also handed us a bonus we will reuse: **in one dimension the subdifferential
is exactly the interval between the left and right derivatives**,
$$
\partial f(x) = \bigl[\,f'_-(x),\,f'_+(x)\,\bigr].
$$ {#eq-1d-subdiff}
and these are the *only* subgradients, by the squeeze argument of @lem-subdiff-gradient
run one-sided: if $g\in\partial f(x)$ then $f(x+t)-f(x)\ge gt$ for all $t$, so dividing by
$t>0$ and letting $t\downarrow 0$ gives $f'_+(x)\ge g$, while dividing by $t<0$ (which
flips the inequality) and letting $t\uparrow 0$ gives $f'_-(x)\le g$; together
$f'_-(x)\le g\le f'_+(x)$, so no $g$ outside the interval qualifies. The two endpoints are
the extreme supporting slopes, and everything between them supports too (by the convexity
of $\partial f$, @lem-subdiff-closed-convex). This single formula computes the
subdifferential of *any* convex function of one variable, and we use it now.

## The subdifferential of the absolute value {#sec-abs}

Apply @eq-1d-subdiff to $f(x)=|x|$, the function the whole article is built around.

- **For $x>0$:** near a positive point $f(x)=x$ is smooth with derivative $+1$, so
  $f'_-(x)=f'_+(x)=1$ and $\partial f(x)=\{+1\}$.
- **For $x<0$:** near a negative point $f(x)=-x$ is smooth with derivative $-1$, so
  $\partial f(x)=\{-1\}$.
- **At $x=0$:** the left derivative is $f'_-(0)=-1$ (approaching from the left, $f(x)=-x$
  has slope $-1$) and the right derivative is $f'_+(0)=+1$. By @eq-1d-subdiff the
  subdifferential is the whole interval between them.

Collecting the three cases:
$$
\boxed{\;
\partial |x| =
\begin{cases}
\{+1\}, & x>0,\\[2pt]
[-1,1], & x=0,\\[2pt]
\{-1\}, & x<0.
\end{cases}
\;}
$$ {#eq-abs-subdiff}

This is the single most-used subdifferential in statistical learning, so it is worth saying
in words: **away from the corner, the subgradient is just the sign of $x$; at the corner it
is the entire interval $[-1,1]$.** The "fat" value $[-1,1]$ at the origin — a set, not a
number — is the object that, three sections from now, produces the lasso's exact zeros.
Notice it contains $0$: by the optimality condition we are about to prove, that is the
algebraic statement that $x=0$ minimizes $|x|$.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What's the subdifferential of $|x|$ at $0$, and why is it an interval?"* The interval
$[-1,1]$. It is an interval (not a stray pair of slopes $\pm 1$) for two reasons you should
be able to give. **Geometrically:** every line through the origin with slope between $-1$
and $+1$ stays under the V (@fig-fan), so every such slope is a valid supporting slope.
**Structurally:** the subdifferential is always closed and convex (@lem-subdiff-closed-convex),
and the only closed convex subsets of $\mathbb R$ are intervals — so once $-1$ and $+1$ are
in, everything between them must be too. The endpoints are the left and right derivatives,
@eq-1d-subdiff. A common wrong answer is "$\{-1,+1\}$"; that set is not convex and would
falsely claim no flat supporting line exists, which would contradict $0$ being the minimum.
:::

@fig-abs-subdiff plots @eq-abs-subdiff as a graph of the *set-valued* map $x\mapsto\partial|x|$:
a horizontal line at $+1$ to the right, at $-1$ to the left, joined by a *vertical segment*
covering all of $[-1,1]$ at $x=0$. That vertical jump — the multivaluedness — is the visual
signature of a kink.

```{python}
#| label: fig-abs-subdiff
#| fig-cap: 'The subdifferential of $|x|$ as a set-valued map. For $x\\neq 0$ it is the single value $\\operatorname{sign}(x)=\\pm 1$ (the ordinary derivative). At the kink $x=0$ it explodes into the entire vertical segment $[-1,1]$ (green). Because that segment **contains $0$**, the point $x=0$ satisfies the optimality condition $0\\in\\partial f(0)$ — the algebraic certificate that it is the minimum. The vertical jump is the fingerprint of a non-differentiable point.'
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
xn = np.linspace(-2.2, -0.001, 100)
xp = np.linspace(0.001, 2.2, 100)
ax.plot(xn, -np.ones_like(xn), color=SUPP, lw=2.6)
ax.plot(xp,  np.ones_like(xp), color=SUPP, lw=2.6)
ax.plot([0, 0], [-1, 1], color=ACCENT, lw=4.0, solid_capstyle="round",
        label=r"$\partial|x|$ at $x=0$ is $[-1,1]$")
ax.scatter([0], [0], color="k", s=45, zorder=6)
ax.annotate("contains $0$\n$\\Rightarrow x=0$ is optimal", xy=(0, 0), xytext=(0.35, -0.15),
            fontsize=10, color=ACCENT)
ax.axhline(0, color="k", lw=0.6)
ax.set_ylim(-1.8, 1.8)
ax.set_xlabel("$x$"); ax.set_ylabel(r"slope $g\in\partial|x|$")
ax.set_title(r"The set-valued derivative $x\mapsto\partial|x|$")
ax.legend(loc="lower right", framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## The general piecewise-linear case: a max of lines {#sec-max}

Before the calculus rules, one more worked subdifferential, because it covers the hinge
loss, the $\ell_\infty$ norm, and any function built as a maximum of linear pieces. Let
$$
f(x) = \max_{1\le i\le m}\,\bigl(a_i^\top x + b_i\bigr),
$$
the pointwise maximum of $m$ affine functions. (This is convex: a max of convex functions
is convex, a fact from [OPTM-04]. The graph is a "fan vault" — flat panels meeting at
ridges.) At a point $x$, let
$$
I(x) = \bigl\{\,i : a_i^\top x + b_i = f(x)\,\bigr\}
$$
be the set of pieces that are **active** — the lines actually achieving the maximum there.
Then the subdifferential is the **convex hull of the active slopes**:
$$
\partial f(x) = \operatorname{conv}\{\,a_i : i\in I(x)\,\}.
$$ {#eq-max-subdiff}

Let us prove the two halves separately at the floor's level.

::: {.proof}
**(Every active slope is a subgradient.)** Fix $i\in I(x)$, so $a_i^\top x + b_i = f(x)$.
For any $y$, the max is at least the $i$-th piece: $f(y)\ge a_i^\top y + b_i$. Subtract the
equality at $x$:
$$
f(y)-f(x) \;\ge\; (a_i^\top y+b_i)-(a_i^\top x + b_i) \;=\; a_i^\top(y-x).
$$
That is exactly @eq-subgrad with $g=a_i$, so each active $a_i\in\partial f(x)$. Because
$\partial f(x)$ is convex (@lem-subdiff-closed-convex), it contains every convex
combination of the active $a_i$ as well — i.e. it contains
$\operatorname{conv}\{a_i:i\in I(x)\}$.

**(Nothing outside that hull is a subgradient.)** Suppose $g$ is a subgradient and fix any
direction $d$. We first compute how $f$ changes along $d$, then squeeze $g$ with it. Each
inactive piece sits *strictly* below $f(x)$ at $x$: for $i\notin I(x)$, the gap
$\delta_i = f(x)-(a_i^\top x+b_i)>0$. Since there are finitely many pieces, let
$\delta=\min_{i\notin I(x)}\delta_i>0$. Along the ray $x+td$, each linear piece changes by
the bounded amount $t\,a_i^\top d$, so for $t$ small enough that
$t\max_i|a_i^\top d| < \delta/2$, every inactive piece stays below the active ones and the
maximum is achieved *only* among active pieces:
$$
f(x+td)=\max_{i\in I(x)}\bigl(a_i^\top x+b_i+t\,a_i^\top d\bigr)
       =f(x)+t\,\max_{i\in I(x)} a_i^\top d,
$$
using $a_i^\top x+b_i=f(x)$ for active $i$. Hence the one-sided difference quotient is
*exactly* $\frac{f(x+td)-f(x)}{t}=\max_{i\in I(x)} a_i^\top d$ for all small $t>0$, so the
**directional derivative** is $f'(x;d)=\max_{i\in I(x)} a_i^\top d$. Now the subgradient
inequality @eq-subgrad with $y=x+td$ gives $f(x+td)-f(x)\ge t\,g^\top d$; dividing by $t>0$
and letting $t\downarrow 0$ (exactly the squeeze of @lem-subdiff-gradient) forces
$g^\top d\le \max_{i\in I(x)} a_i^\top d$ for every $d$. A vector $g$ satisfying
$g^\top d\le \max_{i\in I(x)} a_i^\top d$ for all $d$ is, by the (imported)
**support-function characterization of a convex hull**, exactly a member of
$\operatorname{conv}\{a_i:i\in I(x)\}$ — the support function of a compact convex set equals
that of its hull, and equality of support functions means equality of sets. Hence no $g$
outside the hull qualifies. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

@eq-max-subdiff specializes back to $|x|=\max(x,-x)$ instantly: the two pieces have slopes
$a_1=+1,a_2=-1$. For $x>0$ only the first is active, $\partial=\{1\}$; for $x<0$ only the
second, $\partial=\{-1\}$; at $x=0$ both are active and the hull of $\{+1,-1\}$ is the
interval $[-1,1]$ — recovering @eq-abs-subdiff. The same rule gives the subdifferential of
the hinge loss and the $\ell_\infty$ norm; we leave those to the worked examples and the
exercises a reader would do.

# The calculus of subgradients {#sec-calculus}

A single subdifferential is of limited use; the power of ordinary calculus comes from its
*rules* — the derivative of a sum is the sum of derivatives, constants pull out, the chain
rule composes. We need the convex analogues, because the objectives we minimize are built
by adding a smooth loss to a non-smooth penalty (lasso = $\tfrac12\|y-X\beta\|^2 +
\lambda\|\beta\|_1$) and by composing norms with affine maps. We prove the three rules we
will actually use; each is the inequality @eq-subgrad pushed through the operation.

## Nonnegative scaling {#sec-scaling}

::: {#lem-scaling}
**Lemma (scaling).** For a convex $f$ and a constant $\alpha\ge 0$,
$\partial(\alpha f)(x) = \alpha\,\partial f(x) = \{\alpha g : g\in\partial f(x)\}$.
:::

::: {.proof}
If $g\in\partial f(x)$ then $f(y)\ge f(x)+g^\top(y-x)$. Multiply both sides by $\alpha\ge 0$
(legal — multiplying an inequality by a nonnegative number preserves its direction):
$\alpha f(y)\ge \alpha f(x)+(\alpha g)^\top(y-x)$, which says $\alpha g\in\partial(\alpha f)(x)$.
Conversely if $\alpha>0$ and $h\in\partial(\alpha f)(x)$, divide by $\alpha$ to get
$h/\alpha\in\partial f(x)$, so $h=\alpha(h/\alpha)\in\alpha\,\partial f(x)$. (For $\alpha=0$
both sides are $\{0\}$.) $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This is why the penalty weight $\lambda$ in $\lambda|x|$ simply scales the interval:
$\partial(\lambda|x|)$ at $0$ is $\lambda\cdot[-1,1]=[-\lambda,\lambda]$. Hold that; it sets
the lasso's threshold.

## The sum rule {#sec-sum}

::: {#thm-sum}
**Theorem (sum rule).** For convex functions $f$ and $h$ (finite everywhere),
$$
\partial(f+h)(x) \;=\; \partial f(x) + \partial h(x)
\;=\;\{\,g_f+g_h : g_f\in\partial f(x),\ g_h\in\partial h(x)\,\},
$$
the **Minkowski sum** of the two subdifferentials.
:::

::: {.proof}
We prove the easy inclusion $\supseteq$ fully — it is the only direction the optimality
proofs need — and flag the reverse. Take $g_f\in\partial f(x)$ and $g_h\in\partial h(x)$.
By definition, for all $y$,
$$
f(y)\ge f(x)+g_f^\top(y-x),\qquad h(y)\ge h(x)+g_h^\top(y-x).
$$
Add the two inequalities (adding two true inequalities of the same direction is always
valid):
$$
f(y)+h(y)\;\ge\; \bigl(f(x)+h(x)\bigr) + (g_f+g_h)^\top(y-x).
$$
That is exactly @eq-subgrad for the function $f+h$ with subgradient $g_f+g_h$, so
$g_f+g_h\in\partial(f+h)(x)$. Hence $\partial f(x)+\partial h(x)\subseteq\partial(f+h)(x)$.
The reverse inclusion $\subseteq$ — that *every* subgradient of the sum decomposes this way
— is the **Moreau–Rockafellar theorem**; it requires a mild regularity condition (here
satisfied automatically because $f,h$ are finite everywhere) and a separation argument we
import from convex analysis rather than rebuild. With finite convex summands the two sets
coincide. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The $\supseteq$ direction is the workhorse: it lets us *build* a subgradient of a sum by
adding a subgradient of each part. For the lasso objective $f(\beta)+h(\beta)$ with $f$
smooth and $h=\lambda\|\beta\|_1$, the smooth part contributes its single gradient
$\nabla f$ (by @lem-subdiff-gradient) and the penalty contributes its interval-valued
subdifferential, and we add them. That is precisely the move @sec-softthresh makes.

## Separability: the $\ell_1$ norm {#sec-l1}

The lasso penalty is $\|\beta\|_1=\sum_{j=1}^p |\beta_j|$ — a **separable** sum, each term
depending on its own coordinate. Separability makes its subdifferential a clean product.

::: {#lem-l1}
**Lemma (subdifferential of the $\ell_1$ norm).** For
$\|\beta\|_1=\sum_{j=1}^p|\beta_j|$, the subdifferential at $\beta$ is the box-like set
$$
\partial\|\beta\|_1 = \bigl\{\,g\in\mathbb R^p : g_j=\operatorname{sign}(\beta_j)\text{ if }\beta_j\neq 0,\ \ g_j\in[-1,1]\text{ if }\beta_j=0\,\bigr\}.
$$ {#eq-l1-subdiff}
Coordinatewise: each $g_j\in\partial|\beta_j|$, with the active coordinates pinned to their
sign and the zero coordinates free to roam in $[-1,1]$.
:::

::: {.proof}
Write $\|\beta\|_1=\sum_j h_j(\beta)$ where $h_j(\beta)=|\beta_j|$ depends only on the
$j$-th coordinate. Each $h_j$ is convex, so the sum rule (@thm-sum, applied $p-1$ times)
gives $\partial\|\beta\|_1=\sum_j \partial h_j(\beta)$. Now $h_j$ as a function on
$\mathbb R^p$ is $|\beta_j|$ composed with "pick coordinate $j$." Its subgradient set is
therefore all vectors that are $\partial|\cdot|$ in the $j$-th slot and $0$ elsewhere: if
$g\in\partial h_j(\beta)$ then for the perturbation $y=\beta+te_k$ in any coordinate $k$,
the inequality $h_j(y)\ge h_j(\beta)+g^\top(y-\beta)$ becomes $|\beta_j + t[k=j]|\ge
|\beta_j| + t\,g_k$; for $k\neq j$ this forces $g_k=0$ (the left side does not move), and
for $k=j$ it is exactly the scalar condition $g_j\in\partial|\beta_j|$ from @eq-abs-subdiff.
Summing these single-coordinate sets (Minkowski sum) over $j$ stacks one independent
$\partial|\beta_j|$ into each coordinate — giving precisely @eq-l1-subdiff. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

In words, exactly as we will need it: **at a coordinate that is currently nonzero, the
$\ell_1$ subgradient there is locked to $\pm 1$ (the sign); at a coordinate sitting at zero,
the subgradient there is a free dial in $[-1,1]$.** That free dial is the slack that lets a
coordinate *stay* at zero — the seed of sparsity.

## The affine chain rule {#sec-chain}

One last rule, because penalties and losses usually act on a *linear transform* of the
variable (think $\|y - X\beta\|_1$, an $\ell_1$ loss on the residual $X\beta$, or a norm of
$A x + b$).

::: {#lem-chain}
**Lemma (affine composition).** Let $f(x) = h(Ax+b)$ with $h$ convex, $A$ a matrix, $b$ a
vector. Then
$$
\partial f(x) \;\supseteq\; A^\top\,\partial h(Ax+b) \;=\;\{\,A^\top g : g\in\partial h(Ax+b)\,\},
$$
with equality under the same mild regularity as the sum rule (here, always).
:::

::: {.proof}
Take $g\in\partial h(Ax+b)$, so $h(z)\ge h(Ax+b)+g^\top(z-(Ax+b))$ for all $z$. Specialize
$z=Ay+b$ for an arbitrary $y$ (a legal choice, since $z$ ranges over all of its space as
$y$ does over its domain along the affine image):
$$
h(Ay+b)\;\ge\; h(Ax+b) + g^\top\bigl((Ay+b)-(Ax+b)\bigr) = f(x) + g^\top A(y-x).
$$
Now use the transpose identity $g^\top A(y-x) = (A^\top g)^\top(y-x)$ (this is just the
definition of the transpose: $g^\top A v = (A^\top g)^\top v$). The left side is $f(y)$.
So $f(y)\ge f(x)+(A^\top g)^\top(y-x)$, which is @eq-subgrad with subgradient $A^\top g$.
Hence $A^\top g\in\partial f(x)$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

These four rules — scaling, sum, separability, affine composition — are enough to assemble
the subdifferential of essentially every regularized objective in this series. We now use
them for the one result that justifies the whole article.

# The optimality condition: zero is a subgradient {#sec-optimality}

This is the destination. For a smooth convex function, $x^\star$ is the global minimum iff
$\nabla f(x^\star)=\mathbf 0$. The non-smooth replacement is breathtakingly clean: **$x^\star$
is the global minimum iff $\mathbf 0$ is one of the subgradients there.** And unlike the
smooth first-order condition, which is only *necessary* in general and needs a second-order
test to confirm a minimum, the convex subgradient condition is **necessary and sufficient
and global**, with a one-line proof.

::: {#thm-optimality}
**Theorem (subgradient optimality, Fermat's rule for convex functions).** Let $f$ be
convex. Then $x^\star$ is a **global** minimizer of $f$ if and only if
$$
\mathbf 0 \in \partial f(x^\star).
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
The proof is a tautology once you read the definition correctly — that is the beauty of it.
By @eq-subdiff, the statement "$\mathbf 0\in\partial f(x^\star)$" means: $\mathbf 0$ is a
subgradient at $x^\star$, i.e.
$$
f(y)\;\ge\; f(x^\star) + \mathbf 0^\top(y-x^\star) \;=\; f(x^\star)\qquad\text{for all }y.
$$
But "$f(y)\ge f(x^\star)$ for all $y$" is *exactly the definition* of $x^\star$ being a
global minimizer of $f$. So the two statements are literally the same statement. No
convexity was even needed for *this* equivalence — but convexity is what guarantees (via
@lem-nonempty) that a subdifferential exists to test, and it is what makes this a *global*
statement rather than a local one. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

That deserves a pause. The smooth condition $\nabla f=\mathbf 0$ required an argument
(steepest descent, the tangent plane) to connect "flat" to "optimal," and even then it only
gave a stationary point. The subgradient condition $\mathbf 0\in\partial f$ *is* optimality,
unpacked — because the subgradient inequality was *defined* as a global lower bound. The
flat supporting plane through $(x^\star,f(x^\star))$ literally says "the entire graph sits
on or above this height," which is what "minimum" means. @fig-optimality makes it visible
for $|x|$: at any nonzero point the only supporting line is sloped, so it cuts below the
function on one side and cannot certify optimality; at the origin the *flat* line is a valid
support, certifying the minimum.

```{python}
#| label: fig-optimality
#| fig-cap: 'The optimality condition $0\\in\\partial f$ for $f(x)=|x|$. **Left:** at a non-optimal point $x_0=1$ the only supporting line has slope $+1$ (the derivative); no flat line supports the graph there, so $0\\notin\\partial f(1)$ and the point is not optimal. **Right:** at $x=0$ the flat line $y=0$ (slope $0$) is a legitimate supporting line — it lies under the whole graph — so $0\\in\\partial f(0)=[-1,1]$, certifying $x=0$ as the global minimum. Optimality is exactly ''a horizontal supporting line exists.'''
x = np.linspace(-2.2, 2.2, 400)
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.6, 3.8), sharey=True)

axL.plot(x, np.abs(x), color=FUNC, lw=2.4, label=r"$|x|$")
x0 = 1.0
axL.plot(x, 1.0 * (x - x0) + abs(x0), color=SUPP, lw=1.8, label="supporting line, slope $1$")
axL.scatter([x0], [abs(x0)], color="k", s=45, zorder=5)
axL.set_title(r"At $x_0=1$: only sloped support, $0\notin\partial f$")
axL.set_xlabel("$x$"); axL.set_ylabel("$y$")
axL.legend(loc="upper center", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)

axR.plot(x, np.abs(x), color=FUNC, lw=2.4, label=r"$|x|$")
axR.axhline(0, color=ACCENT, lw=2.0, label="flat support, slope $0$")
axR.scatter([0], [0], color="k", s=45, zorder=5)
axR.annotate(r"$0\in\partial f(0)$" "\nminimum certified", xy=(0, 0),
             xytext=(0.3, 1.1), fontsize=9.5, color=ACCENT)
axR.set_title(r"At $x=0$: flat support exists, $0\in\partial f$")
axR.set_xlabel("$x$")
axR.legend(loc="upper center", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"How does $\mathbf 0\in\partial f(x^\star)$ relate to $\nabla f(x^\star)=\mathbf 0$, and why
is the subgradient version automatically global while the gradient version needs a
second-order check?"* Two parts.
**Relation:** where $f$ is differentiable, $\partial f=\{\nabla f\}$ (@lem-subdiff-gradient),
so $\mathbf 0\in\partial f$ collapses to $\nabla f=\mathbf 0$ — it is a strict generalization,
identical on smooth functions, with extra reach at kinks.
**Global vs. needing a check:** the gradient condition $\nabla f=\mathbf 0$ is *stationarity*,
a *local, first-order* statement that is satisfied by minima, maxima, and saddles alike — you
need the Hessian (a second-order check) to tell which, *unless* you already know $f$ is convex.
The subgradient condition $\mathbf 0\in\partial f$ has the global lower bound *built into its
definition* (@eq-subgrad is "$f(y)\ge$ everywhere"), so for convex $f$ it certifies a global
minimum directly, no second-order anything. The cost of that power is that it only works for
convex functions; for non-convex $f$, $\mathbf 0\in\partial f$ (suitably generalized) is again
just a necessary local condition.
:::

# Cashing it in: the lasso and soft-thresholding {#sec-softthresh}

We now spend the optimality condition on the result the Regression track links here for and
that [OPTM-10] and [OPTM-16] both build on: the closed-form solution of the **scalar lasso
problem**. Every coordinate update of a lasso solver is one instance of it.

The problem is
$$
\min_{\beta\in\mathbb R}\ \phi(\beta),\qquad
\phi(\beta) = \tfrac12(\beta-\rho)^2 + \lambda|\beta|,
$$ {#eq-scalar-lasso}
with a fixed target $\rho\in\mathbb R$ and penalty $\lambda> 0$. Read it as a tug-of-war:
the quadratic $\tfrac12(\beta-\rho)^2$ wants $\beta=\rho$ (stay near the data's preferred
value $\rho$), while $\lambda|\beta|$ wants $\beta=0$ (be sparse), paying $\lambda$ per unit
of $|\beta|$. (In the multivariate lasso, $\rho$ is the correlation of one predictor with
the current partial residual; this scalar problem is the per-coordinate subproblem solved in
[OPTM-10]. Here we treat it on its own.)

$\phi$ is convex — a sum of a convex quadratic and the convex $\lambda|\beta|$ — so by
@thm-optimality the minimizer $\beta^\star$ is characterized *exactly* by
$0\in\partial\phi(\beta^\star)$. Build $\partial\phi$ with the calculus: the smooth part has
gradient $(\beta-\rho)$ (by @lem-subdiff-gradient its subdifferential is the singleton
$\{\beta-\rho\}$), and the penalty contributes $\lambda\,\partial|\beta|$ (by @lem-scaling).
By the sum rule (@thm-sum),
$$
\partial\phi(\beta) = (\beta-\rho) + \lambda\,\partial|\beta|.
$$
The optimality condition $0\in\partial\phi(\beta)$ splits into the three cases of
@eq-abs-subdiff. We solve each in full.

**Case $\beta>0$.** Here $\partial|\beta|=\{+1\}$, so the condition is the single equation
$0 = (\beta-\rho)+\lambda$, giving $\beta=\rho-\lambda$. This is *self-consistent* (we
assumed $\beta>0$) precisely when $\rho-\lambda>0$, i.e. $\rho>\lambda$. So: if
$\rho>\lambda$, the minimizer is $\beta^\star=\rho-\lambda$.

**Case $\beta<0$.** Here $\partial|\beta|=\{-1\}$, so $0=(\beta-\rho)-\lambda$, giving
$\beta=\rho+\lambda$. Self-consistent ($\beta<0$) iff $\rho+\lambda<0$, i.e. $\rho<-\lambda$.
So: if $\rho<-\lambda$, the minimizer is $\beta^\star=\rho+\lambda$.

**Case $\beta=0$.** Here $\partial|\beta|=[-1,1]$, so the condition $0\in\partial\phi(0)$
reads
$$
0 \in (0-\rho) + \lambda[-1,1] = [-\rho-\lambda,\ -\rho+\lambda].
$$
A number lies in an interval $[a,b]$ iff $a\le 0\le b$, here $-\rho-\lambda\le 0$ and
$-\rho+\lambda\ge 0$, i.e. $-\lambda\le\rho\le\lambda$, i.e. $|\rho|\le\lambda$. So: whenever
the target $\rho$ is **smaller in magnitude than the penalty $\lambda$**, the minimizer is
*exactly* $\beta^\star=0$.

The three cases are exhaustive and mutually exclusive ($\rho>\lambda$, $|\rho|\le\lambda$,
$\rho<-\lambda$ partition the line), so collecting them gives the closed-form minimizer, the
**soft-thresholding operator**:
$$
\boxed{\;
\beta^\star = S_\lambda(\rho) =
\operatorname{sign}(\rho)\,\bigl(|\rho|-\lambda\bigr)_+ =
\begin{cases}
\rho-\lambda, & \rho>\lambda,\\[2pt]
0, & |\rho|\le\lambda,\\[2pt]
\rho+\lambda, & \rho<-\lambda,
\end{cases}
\;}
$$ {#eq-soft}
where $(z)_+=\max(0,z)$. In one phrase: **pull $\rho$ toward zero by $\lambda$, and snap to
exactly zero anything already within $\lambda$ of zero.** @fig-soft draws it.

```{python}
#| label: fig-soft
#| fig-cap: 'Soft-thresholding $S_\\lambda(\\rho)=\\operatorname{sign}(\\rho)(|\\rho|-\\lambda)_+$ (red), the exact minimizer of the scalar lasso $\\tfrac12(\\beta-\\rho)^2+\\lambda|\\beta|$, derived from $0\\in\\partial\\phi$. It is the identity (grey dashed) shifted toward zero by $\\lambda$, with a flat **dead zone** $|\\rho|\\le\\lambda$ where the answer is exactly $0$. That dead zone — produced by the interval $[-1,1]$ in $\\partial|\\beta|$ — is *why the lasso zeroes coefficients*. The smooth ridge solution $\\rho/(1+2\\lambda)$ (green) only shrinks proportionally and never reaches zero: no kink, no sparsity.'
def soft(rho, lam):
    return np.sign(rho) * np.maximum(np.abs(rho) - lam, 0.0)

rho = np.linspace(-3, 3, 600)
lam = 1.0
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8, 4.6))
ax.plot(rho, rho, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.4, label=r"identity $\beta=\rho$ (no penalty)")
ax.plot(rho, soft(rho, lam), color=SUPP, lw=2.6, label=r"soft-threshold $S_\lambda(\rho)$ (lasso)")
ax.plot(rho, rho / (1 + 2 * lam), color=ACCENT, lw=1.8, alpha=0.85,
        label=r"ridge $\rho/(1+2\lambda)$ (no kink)")
ax.axvspan(-lam, lam, color=GREY, alpha=0.12)
ax.text(0, 2.3, "dead zone\n$|\\rho|\\leq\\lambda$", ha="center", fontsize=9, color=GREY)
ax.axhline(0, color="k", lw=0.6); ax.axvline(0, color="k", lw=0.6)
ax.set_xlabel(r"target $\rho$")
ax.set_ylabel(r"minimizer $\beta^\star$")
ax.set_title(r"Soft-thresholding from $0\in\partial\phi$  ($\lambda=1$)")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.95)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The dead zone is the whole story, and it traces straight back to @eq-abs-subdiff. **The
sparsity of the lasso is the interval $[-1,1]$ in $\partial|\beta|$, made operational.**
Contrast the ridge penalty $\lambda\beta^2$: redo the derivation and there is *no kink*, so
the optimality condition is the ordinary equation $(\beta-\rho)+2\lambda\beta=0$, giving
$\beta^\star=\rho/(1+2\lambda)$ — proportional shrinkage that pulls toward zero but reaches
it only in the limit $\lambda\to\infty$. Exact zeros require the corner; the corner requires
subgradients; subgradients are this article. The circle is closed.

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We now run the machinery on three honest problems — one we can check by hand against the
formula, one a real sparse-recovery task, and one at the edge of applicability where the
naive use of subgradients fails and tells us to reach for something else.

## Example 1 — soft-thresholding a single coefficient, by hand and by code {#sec-ex-scalar}

Take the scalar lasso @eq-scalar-lasso with a concrete target and penalty and walk every
step. We verify the closed form @eq-soft against a brute-force grid minimization — the
formula and the numerics must agree, and the optimality condition $0\in\partial\phi$ must
hold at the answer.

```{python}
#| label: fig-scalar-check
#| fig-cap: 'Solving the scalar lasso $\\phi(\\beta)=\\tfrac12(\\beta-\\rho)^2+\\lambda|\\beta|$ three ways for $\\rho=1.8,\\ \\lambda=0.7$. The objective (blue) has a kink at $\\beta=0$; its minimizer from the closed form $S_\\lambda(\\rho)$ (red dot) agrees with a fine-grid brute-force search (black ring). The subgradient set $\\partial\\phi(\\beta^\\star)=(\\beta^\\star-\\rho)+\\lambda\\,\\partial|\\beta^\\star|$ contains $0$, the printed certificate of optimality.'
def phi(beta, rho, lam):
    return 0.5 * (beta - rho) ** 2 + lam * np.abs(beta)

rho_val, lam_val = 1.8, 0.7
beta_grid = np.linspace(-1.5, 3.0, 20001)
phi_grid = phi(beta_grid, rho_val, lam_val)
beta_brute = beta_grid[np.argmin(phi_grid)]
beta_soft = np.sign(rho_val) * max(abs(rho_val) - lam_val, 0.0)

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(beta_grid, phi_grid, color=FUNC, lw=2.2, label=r"$\phi(\beta)$")
ax.scatter([beta_soft], [phi(beta_soft, rho_val, lam_val)], color=SUPP, s=80,
           zorder=5, label=fr"closed form $S_\lambda(\rho)={beta_soft:.2f}$")
ax.scatter([beta_brute], [phi(beta_brute, rho_val, lam_val)], facecolors="none",
           edgecolors="k", s=160, lw=1.6, zorder=4, label=f"brute force = {beta_brute:.2f}")
ax.axvline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.8, ls=":")
ax.set_xlabel(r"$\beta$"); ax.set_ylabel(r"$\phi(\beta)$")
ax.set_title(r"Scalar lasso: closed form vs. brute force")
ax.legend(loc="upper center", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.95)
plt.tight_layout()

# Optimality certificate: 0 in partial phi(beta*) = (beta*-rho) + lam*partial|beta*|
smooth_part = beta_soft - rho_val
if beta_soft > 0:
    subdiff = [smooth_part + lam_val]            # singleton {+1}
elif beta_soft < 0:
    subdiff = [smooth_part - lam_val]            # singleton {-1}
else:
    subdiff = [smooth_part - lam_val, smooth_part + lam_val]  # interval endpoints
print(f"rho = {rho_val}, lambda = {lam_val}")
print(f"closed-form minimizer S_lambda(rho) = {beta_soft:.4f}")
print(f"brute-force minimizer                = {beta_brute:.4f}")
lo, hi = (subdiff[0], subdiff[0]) if len(subdiff) == 1 else (subdiff[0], subdiff[1])
print(f"subdifferential at minimizer = [{lo:.4f}, {hi:.4f}]  -> contains 0: {lo <= 0 <= hi}")
```

By hand: $\rho=1.8>\lambda=0.7$, so we are in the first case of @eq-soft and
$\beta^\star=\rho-\lambda=1.8-0.7=1.1$, matching the figure. The certificate at the bottom
confirms $0\in\partial\phi(1.1)=\{(1.1-1.8)+0.7\}=\{0\}$ — the subgradient is *exactly* zero
because the minimizer is at a smooth point. Now lower the target below the penalty: with
$\rho=0.5<\lambda=0.7$ the second case fires and $\beta^\star=0$, and there the
subdifferential is the *interval* $(0-0.5)+0.7[-1,1]=[-1.2,0.2]$, which contains $0$ — the
optimality is certified by the corner, not by a single vanishing derivative. That is the
qualitative difference between shrinkage and selection, visible in one number.

## Example 2 — sparse signal recovery with the subgradient certificate {#sec-ex-sparse}

A genuine use case: we observe a noisy, *underdetermined* linear measurement of a sparse
signal and recover it by the lasso. This is the regime of compressed sensing and
high-dimensional regression — more unknowns than equations, rescued by the prior belief
that the truth is sparse. We solve $\min_\beta \tfrac{1}{2}\|y-X\beta\|_2^2 +
\lambda\|\beta\|_1$ by coordinate-wise soft-thresholding (the algorithm [OPTM-10] is built
on, used here as a black box to *exhibit* the subgradient theory), and then **verify the
solution with the optimality condition $\mathbf 0\in\partial f(\beta^\star)$** — the global
multivariate version, which is the article's headline put to work.

```{python}
#| label: fig-sparse
#| fig-cap: 'Lasso recovery of a sparse signal ($n=80$ measurements, $p=160$ unknowns, only $5$ truly nonzero). **Left:** recovered coefficients (red stems) against the ground truth (green circles); the $155$ irrelevant coordinates are driven to *exactly* zero and the $5$ true spikes are recovered (slightly shrunk — the honest price of the $\\ell_1$ penalty). **Right:** the subgradient certificate. For each coordinate we plot the required subgradient $g_j=-[\\nabla(\\tfrac12\\|y-X\\beta\\|^2)]_j/\\lambda$ implied by optimality; the theory demands $g_j=\\operatorname{sign}(\\beta_j)$ on active coordinates and $g_j\\in[-1,1]$ on zero coordinates. Every point lands where @eq-l1-subdiff says it must — the numerical solution satisfies $\\mathbf 0\\in\\partial f$ to tolerance.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n, p, k = 80, 160, 5
X = rng.standard_normal((n, p))
X /= np.linalg.norm(X, axis=0) / np.sqrt(n)   # standardize columns: (1/n)||x_j||^2 = 1
beta_true = np.zeros(p)
support = rng.choice(p, size=k, replace=False)
beta_true[support] = rng.uniform(2.0, 4.0, size=k) * rng.choice([-1, 1], size=k)
y = X @ beta_true + 0.1 * rng.standard_normal(n)

def soft(z, t):
    return np.sign(z) * np.maximum(np.abs(z) - t, 0.0)

# Coordinate descent (the OPTM-10 engine), used here only to produce a solution to certify.
lam = 0.25
beta = np.zeros(p)
for _ in range(400):
    for j in range(p):
        r_partial = y - X @ beta + X[:, j] * beta[j]
        rho_j = (X[:, j] @ r_partial) / n
        beta[j] = soft(rho_j, lam)

# Optimality certificate: at optimum, -grad_smooth in lambda * partial||beta||_1, i.e.
# g_j := -(1/n) X_j^T (X beta - y) / lambda  must satisfy g_j = sign(beta_j) (active)
#        and |g_j| <= 1 (zero coords).
grad_smooth = (X.T @ (X @ beta - y)) / n
g = -grad_smooth / lam
active = np.abs(beta) > 1e-6
max_violation_active = np.max(np.abs(g[active] - np.sign(beta[active]))) if active.any() else 0.0
max_violation_zero = np.max(np.abs(g[~active])) if (~active).any() else 0.0

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.0, 3.8))
axL.stem(np.arange(p), beta, linefmt=f"{SUPP}", markerfmt="o", basefmt=" ")
axL.scatter(support, beta_true[support], facecolors="none", edgecolors=ACCENT, s=90,
            lw=1.8, zorder=5, label="true nonzeros")
axL.set_xlabel("coordinate $j$"); axL.set_ylabel(r"$\beta_j$")
axL.set_title(f"Recovered signal ({int(active.sum())} nonzeros of {p})")
axL.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)

axR.axhspan(-1, 1, color=FILL, alpha=0.3, label=r"allowed band $[-1,1]$ at $\beta_j=0$")
axR.scatter(np.arange(p)[~active], g[~active], color=GREY, s=14, label="zero coords")
axR.scatter(np.arange(p)[active], g[active], color=SUPP, s=40, zorder=5, label="active coords")
axR.axhline(1, color="k", lw=0.7, ls=":"); axR.axhline(-1, color="k", lw=0.7, ls=":")
axR.set_xlabel("coordinate $j$"); axR.set_ylabel(r"required subgradient $g_j$")
axR.set_title(r"Subgradient certificate $0\in\partial f$")
axR.legend(loc="lower right", fontsize=8, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

print(f"active coordinates: {int(active.sum())}  (true: {k})")
print(f"max |g_j - sign(beta_j)| on active coords = {max_violation_active:.2e}")
print(f"max |g_j| on zero coords                  = {max_violation_zero:.3f}  (must be <= 1)")
```

The certificate is the point of the example, and it is exactly the multivariate optimality
condition. At the optimum, $\mathbf 0\in\partial f(\beta^\star)$ with $f=\tfrac12\|y-X\beta\|^2
+ \lambda\|\beta\|_1$ unpacks via the sum rule into
$$
\mathbf 0 \in \nabla\bigl(\tfrac12\|y-X\beta\|^2\bigr) + \lambda\,\partial\|\beta\|_1
= X^\top(X\beta-y) + \lambda\,\partial\|\beta\|_1,
$$
so the (scaled) negative smooth gradient $g_j = -[X^\top(X\beta-y)]_j/(n\lambda)$ must be a
member of $\partial|\beta_j|$ coordinate by coordinate (@eq-l1-subdiff): equal to
$\operatorname{sign}(\beta_j)$ wherever $\beta_j\neq 0$, and inside $[-1,1]$ wherever
$\beta_j=0$. The printout confirms both to numerical tolerance, and the right panel shows
every zero coordinate sitting *inside* the band $[-1,1]$ and every active coordinate pinned
to $\pm 1$. **This is how you check a lasso solution without re-running the solver:** the
subgradient condition is a certificate, computable from one gradient evaluation. That is a
practical, interview-grade use of the theory.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Given a candidate lasso solution, how would you verify it is optimal — and explain the
'KKT-style' check practitioners mention?"* Compute the smooth gradient
$\nabla(\tfrac12\|y-X\beta\|^2)=X^\top(X\beta-y)$ and check the **stationarity / subgradient
condition** $\mathbf 0\in\partial f(\beta)$ coordinatewise: on every nonzero coordinate the
gradient must equal $-\lambda\operatorname{sign}(\beta_j)$ (so the implied subgradient is
$\pm 1$), and on every zero coordinate $|[X^\top(X\beta-y)]_j|\le\lambda$ (the implied
subgradient lies in $[-1,1]$). These *are* the KKT conditions for the lasso (the formal KKT
machinery is [OPTM-14]); the "$\le\lambda$ on the zeros" inequality is exactly the interval
$[-1,1]$ of $\partial|0|$ scaled by $\lambda$. If any zero coordinate violates it, that
coordinate *should* enter the model and the solver has not converged. This check is $O(np)$,
far cheaper than another solve, and it is the standard convergence test in `glmnet`-style
solvers.
:::

## Example 3 — the edge of applicability: subgradient descent crawls {#sec-ex-edge}

The honest boundary. Having a subgradient is *not* the same as having a descent direction.
For gradient descent ([OPTM-06]) we step against $\nabla f$ because $-\nabla f$ is
guaranteed to decrease $f$ locally. **A subgradient carries no such guarantee**: at a kink,
some subgradients point in directions that *increase* $f$. So the naive "subgradient
method" — step against an arbitrary subgradient — does not reliably descend; it can only be
made to converge with a *decaying* step size, and even then it crawls and chatters near the
optimum. We show this honestly, minimizing the simplest non-smooth convex function $f(x)=|x|$
two ways and watching the subgradient method oscillate around the kink it cannot settle into.

```{python}
#| label: fig-subgrad-crawl
#| fig-cap: 'Why a subgradient is not a descent direction. Minimizing $f(x)=|x|$ (minimizer $x^\\star=0$). **Left:** the subgradient method $x_{k+1}=x_k-\\eta_k\\,g_k$ with $g_k\\in\\partial|x_k|$ (here $\\operatorname{sign}(x_k)$). With a *constant* step $\\eta$ (grey) it cannot converge — it overshoots zero and bounces between $\\pm\\eta$ forever, because the subgradient is $\\pm 1$ no matter how close to $0$ it gets. Only a *decaying* step $\\eta_k=\\eta_0/\\sqrt{k}$ (red) inches in, and slowly. **Right:** the objective value — the decaying-step run grinds down at the textbook $O(1/\\sqrt k)$ crawl, while the constant-step run plateaus at a noise floor. The fix is not a better step rule but a different algorithm: the **proximal** method of [OPTM-16], which solves the kink exactly via soft-thresholding.'
def subgrad_descent(x0, steps, eta_fn):
    xs = [x0]; fs = [abs(x0)]
    x = x0
    for k in range(1, steps + 1):
        g = np.sign(x)          # a subgradient of |x|; sign(0)=0 is valid but x rarely hits 0
        x = x - eta_fn(k) * g
        xs.append(x); fs.append(abs(x))
    return np.array(xs), np.array(fs)

steps = 200
# Start off-grid so the constant step never lands exactly on the kink:
# it overshoots 0 every step and bounces between +/- ~eta forever.
xs_const, fs_const = subgrad_descent(1.05, steps, lambda k: 0.1)
xs_decay, fs_decay = subgrad_descent(1.05, steps, lambda k: 0.3 / np.sqrt(k))

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.0, 3.8))
axL.axhline(0, color=ACCENT, lw=1.5, ls="--", label=r"optimum $x^\star=0$")
axL.plot(xs_const, color=GREY, lw=1.5, label=r"constant step $\eta=0.1$ (chatters)")
axL.plot(xs_decay, color=SUPP, lw=1.8, label=r"decaying $\eta_k=0.3/\sqrt{k}$")
axL.set_xlabel("iteration $k$"); axL.set_ylabel(r"$x_k$")
axL.set_title("Iterates: constant step never settles")
axL.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)

axR.plot(fs_const, color=GREY, lw=1.5, label="constant step")
axR.plot(fs_decay, color=SUPP, lw=1.8, label="decaying step")
axR.set_xlabel("iteration $k$"); axR.set_ylabel(r"$f(x_k)=|x_k|$")
axR.set_yscale("log")
axR.set_title(r"Objective: a slow $O(1/\sqrt{k})$ crawl at best")
axR.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"constant-step final |x| = {fs_const[-1]:.4f}  (stuck oscillating)")
print(f"decaying-step final |x| = {fs_decay[-1]:.4f}  (slowly approaching 0)")
```

This is the genuinely cautionary picture, not a rigged one. The subgradient method *works*
— it converges with a decaying step — but its rate is the dismal $O(1/\sqrt{k})$ even for
this trivial problem, versus the $O(1/k)$ or linear rates smooth gradient descent enjoys
([OPTM-07]). The reason is structural: near the kink the subgradient stays at full magnitude
$\pm 1$ no matter how close you are, so the method has no built-in slowdown and must be
forced to one by shrinking the step. **When you reach for something else:** almost always.
For the lasso and friends — a *smooth* loss plus a *simple non-smooth* penalty whose prox is
cheap — the right tool is the **proximal-gradient** method ([OPTM-16]): take an ordinary
gradient step on the smooth part, then apply soft-thresholding (the exact solver of the kink,
@eq-soft) to the penalty. It recovers the smooth method's fast rate and produces exact zeros.
Coordinate descent ([OPTM-10]) is the other right tool when the penalty is separable. The
plain subgradient method is the option of last resort, used when the non-smooth part has *no*
exploitable structure — but it is the conceptual foundation those better methods are built on,
which is why we derived it.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**A subgradient is not a descent direction, and $0\in\partial f$ is not "$\partial f=\{0\}$."**
Two errors that sink interview answers and code alike. (1) Stepping against an arbitrary
subgradient can *increase* the objective; only the *negative gradient* (at smooth points) is
a guaranteed descent direction. This is why subgradient descent needs decaying steps and is
slow — do not expect gradient-descent behavior from it. (2) Optimality is $0\in\partial f$
(zero is *one of* the subgradients), **not** $\partial f=\{0\}$ (zero is the *only*
subgradient). At a kink the subdifferential is a whole set; demanding it equal $\{0\}$ would
wrongly reject the minimum of $|x|$, whose subdifferential there is the interval $[-1,1]$.
The condition is *membership*, not *equality*.
:::

# Failure modes and the honest boundary {#sec-failure}

The subgradient theory is exact and complete — *for convex functions*. Its boundaries are
worth stating plainly, because each marks where the clean guarantees evaporate.

**Non-convexity dissolves the guarantee.** Theorem @thm-optimality is biconditional and
global *because $f$ is convex*. Drop convexity — neural-network losses, mixtures, anything
with multiple basins — and the subgradient inequality @eq-subgrad simply has no solutions $g$
at most points (no single global affine minorant touches there), so the subdifferential as we
defined it is *empty*. The field repairs this with weaker, *local* notions (the **Clarke
generalized gradient**, **limiting/Fréchet subdifferentials**), for which "$0\in\partial f$"
recovers only a necessary, local stationarity condition — the non-smooth analogue of "could
be a min, max, or saddle." Everything that made the convex condition a *certificate* is gone.
If your objective is non-convex, do not expect a subgradient to certify a global optimum.

**The subdifferential can still be a single point — and then nothing is new.** If $f$ happens
to be differentiable at $x$, $\partial f(x)=\{\nabla f(x)\}$ (@lem-subdiff-gradient) and the
whole apparatus collapses to ordinary calculus. Subgradients earn their keep *only* at the
kinks; away from them they are an expensive way to write $\nabla f$. The art is recognizing
*where* the kinks are (where the model wants a hard zero, a corner, a max) and applying the
set-valued calculus exactly there.

**Computing the full subdifferential can be hard for complicated compositions.** Our calculus
rules (sum, scaling, affine chain) cover the objectives in this series, but they have
regularity conditions (the Moreau–Rockafellar caveat in @thm-sum), and for deeply nested
non-smooth compositions the subdifferential can be genuinely awkward to write down. In
practice one rarely needs the *whole* set — a *single* subgradient suffices to run a
subgradient or proximal method, and that is usually cheap (e.g. $\operatorname{sign}(x)$ for
$|x|$). Knowing you need only one element, not the entire set, is a practitioner's shortcut.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When is subgradient theory the right framework, and when isn't it?"* The crisp answer:
**Use it** when your objective is **convex** and **non-smooth** at points that matter — an
$\ell_1$ or $\ell_\infty$ penalty, a hinge loss, a max of linear functions, a total-variation
term. There it gives an exact, global optimality certificate ($\mathbf 0\in\partial f$) and,
through soft-thresholding and the prox, fast algorithms. **Don't reach for it** when (a) the
function is smooth — ordinary gradients are simpler and faster; (b) the function is
non-convex — the global guarantee is gone and you want local stationarity tools or a
different strategy entirely; or (c) you were about to run *plain* subgradient descent on a
structured problem — its $O(1/\sqrt k)$ crawl (@sec-ex-edge) is almost always beaten by a
proximal or coordinate method ([OPTM-16], [OPTM-10]) that solves the kink exactly. The
deepest framing: subgradients are the *analysis* (what optimality means at a kink); proximal
methods are the *algorithm* (how to exploit a kink efficiently). You want both, and this
article is the analysis half.
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[OPTM-04 *Convex Functions*]**, which gave the tangent-below inequality
$f(y)\ge f(x)+\nabla f(x)^\top(y-x)$ for smooth convex functions and the headline that local
minima are global. This article kept that inequality and *dropped the uniqueness of the
tangent*, producing the **subgradient**, the **subdifferential** $\partial f$, its calculus,
and the optimality condition $\mathbf 0\in\partial f$ that replaces $\nabla f=\mathbf 0$ at
kinks. This **closes the convexity spine** (OPTM-03–OPTM-05). From here:

- **[OPTM-10 *Coordinate Descent*]** — cashes in the **soft-thresholding** rule @eq-soft as
  the per-coordinate lasso update, making coordinate descent the standard `glmnet` solver.
  The scalar subproblem there *is* @eq-scalar-lasso.
- **[OPTM-16 *Proximal and Projected Methods*]** — the algorithmic answer to the
  @sec-ex-edge crawl. The **proximal operator** of $\lambda\|\cdot\|_1$ *is* soft-thresholding,
  and proximal gradient descent (ISTA/FISTA) is the modern lasso solver that subgradient
  descent could not be. This article is its theoretical foundation.
- **[OPTM-14 *KKT Conditions*]** — the subgradient certificate of @sec-ex-sparse is the
  lasso's KKT condition; OPTM-14 develops the general constrained-optimality machinery of
  which $\mathbf 0\in\partial f$ is the unconstrained, non-smooth case.
- **Regression R15 (lasso)** links here for exactly this content — the *subgradients*
  prerequisite the supply-depot contract promises — to justify why the lasso zeroes
  coefficients and how its solvers work.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The reference.** R. T. Rockafellar, *Convex Analysis* (Princeton, 1970) — the
  subdifferential, the sum rule (Moreau–Rockafellar), and the optimality condition in their
  original, complete form. Dense but definitive.
- **The modern textbook treatment.** Boyd & Vandenberghe, *Convex Optimization* (2004),
  §3 and the subgradient/optimality material; Nesterov, *Lectures on Convex Optimization*,
  for the $O(1/\sqrt k)$ subgradient-method rate and why it is unimprovable in general.
- **The application that made this famous.** Tibshirani, "Regression Shrinkage and Selection
  via the Lasso," *JRSS-B* 58 (1996) — soft-thresholding and sparsity, the downstream payoff
  of @eq-abs-subdiff.
- **Open threads this article deferred.** *How do you minimize $f+g$ efficiently when $g$ is
  non-smooth?* — the proximal operator, [OPTM-16]. *What is the subdifferential of a
  non-convex function?* — Clarke and limiting subdifferentials, a graduate convex-analysis or
  variational-analysis course (Rockafellar & Wets, *Variational Analysis*). *Is $\partial f$
  the right object for constrained problems?* — yes, through the KKT conditions, [OPTM-14].
